issues.description: The left/right over- vs under-estimate rules are stated as absolute laws ('On an INCREASING function, left UNDER-estimates...') with no qualifier that they only hold when the function is MONOTONIC over the ENTIRE interval. A reasonable learner will apply 'left under-estimates' to any curve that is increasing on part of the interval, getting the wrong answer on the very multiple-choice items this section claims to nail. The rules are necessary-but-conditional, presented as unconditional.
issues.fix: Add the qualifier: 'as long as the function is increasing/decreasing across the whole interval — if it turns around, you cannot apply this rule blindly.'

issues.description: Section is titled/anchored to concavity but never states a concavity-based rule, while trapezoidal was just introduced in 05. The trapezoid over/under behavior depends on CONCAVITY (trapezoid over-estimates when concave-up, under-estimates when concave-down), and midpoint is the mirror. Instead the script gives only the vague 'Midpoint usually beats both,' which is not exam-reliable and teaches no decision rule. Learners conflate the monotonicity rule (left/right) with the concavity rule (trapezoid/midpoint).
issues.fix: Add the concavity rule explicitly: trapezoid over-estimates on concave-up and under-estimates on concave-down; midpoint does the opposite. Keep monotonicity (left/right) and concavity (trap/mid) as two separate, clearly-labeled rules.

issues.description: FTC Part 1 is stated only for an upper bound (a-to-x and a-to-g(x)). It omits the variable-LOWER-bound case, where the derivative picks up a NEGATIVE sign (d/dx of integral from g(x) to a of f = -f(g(x))*g'(x)). This is a heavily-tested AP variant, and a learner taught only the upper-bound rule will get the sign wrong every time. The continuity hypothesis on f is also dropped — FTC Part 1 requires f continuous.
issues.fix: Add: if the variable is the lower bound, flip the sign first using the bound-swap property (tie it back to property 2 in section 09); and state that f must be continuous on the interval.

issues.description: 'the sample point... left, right, midpoint, anywhere — they all converge to the same number' is asserted with no integrability/continuity hypothesis. True for the (continuous) functions on the AP exam, but stated as a universal truth it is false and plants a wrong mental model about when Riemann sums converge.
issues.fix: Qualify with 'for the continuous functions you'll see on the AP exam' or 'whenever the function is integrable.'

issues.description: 'That area is how far you actually traveled' equates area-under-velocity with distance. It works only because the gas-pedal velocity is positive, but the script never flags this. The deeper truth — area under a velocity curve is DISPLACEMENT (signed), and 'distance traveled' requires the absolute value when velocity goes negative — is never addressed, and 'signed area' first appears only in the recap (15). This sets up the classic distance-vs-displacement trap with a positive-only example.
issues.fix: Note that this works because velocity is positive here, and foreshadow that signed area / displacement vs. total distance is the subtlety — connect it to the 'signed area' phrase used in the recap.

notes: Numerically the worked examples and FTC Part 2 are clean, but the Riemann estimation rules and FTC Part 1 are stated too absolutely — missing the monotonicity/concavity and variable-lower-bound qualifiers that the AP exam specifically targets.
